meta.title: Mercedes C-Class S204 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes C-Class S204 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C-Class Estate — from the supercharged M271 four-cylinder to the V6 M272 and the OM651 diesel that redefined refinement. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the engine choices that defined the third‑gen C‑Class estate. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C-Class S204 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S204 estate specifically — including the pre‑ and post‑facelift engine changes that transformed this generation.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * OM651.911/913: 95% confidence — inherited from the Model Page's verified figures for the wider Mercedes range. * M272.947/961: 85% confidence — well‑documented M272 V6; pattern data. * M271.950/820/860: 80% confidence — known M271 issues; provisional ratings. * OM646.811: 70% confidence — older engine, phased out early; provisional. * OM642.834: 80% confidence — V6 diesel with known issues; provisional. * M276.957: 75% confidence — newer engine; limited long‑term data.

overview.intro: The S204 is the estate version of the third‑generation Mercedes‑Benz C‑Class (W204), launched in August 2007 as the successor to the S203 estate. Built on the same platform as the W204 saloon, it shared the full engine lineup — from the supercharged M271 four‑cylinder petrols to the V6 M272 and the OM651 diesel family that redefined the C‑Class driving experience. A significant facelift arrived in 2011, bringing new engines including the M276 V6 petrol and OM642 V6 diesel.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W204 (shared with W204 saloon, C204 coupé) • Predecessor: S203 (2001–2007) • Successor: S205 (2014–2021) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W204 (saloon), C204 (coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S204 C‑Class estate generated 1,250+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM651 diesel as the most‑requested powertrain.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.1‑litre diesel, introduced in 2008, replaced the ageing OM646 with a lighter, more efficient design. It's the pick of the S204 range — smooth, torquey, and backed by a strong reliability record when serviced properly.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel without the older engine's compromises.

items.quote: Mercedes' 3.5‑litre V6, introduced with the 2011 facelift, replaced the M272 with a more refined and reliable unit. It delivers 306 bhp with a silky character and strong torque.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want genuine V6 performance from a family estate.

items.quote: The M271 four‑cylinder petrol is known for timing chain stretch and tensioner failure — a problem that can lead to catastrophic engine damage if ignored. The EVO versions (820/860) improved the design but didn't eliminate the risk entirely.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a budget who prioritise low running costs over long‑term reliability.

items.engine: OM642.834 (C350 CDI) — oil cooler seal leak
items.quote: The V6 diesel's oil cooler seals are prone to failure, leaking oil into the V of the engine. Repair requires significant disassembly and can be costly if caught late.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the power of a V6 diesel but can afford the premium maintenance.

items.quote: The mid‑range diesel offers the best balance of performance (170 bhp), economy (up to 60 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the S204 range.

The 3.0‑litre V6 petrol offers 231 bhp with a smooth, characterful delivery. It's often overlooked in favour of the diesels but offers a genuinely rewarding driving experience with fewer of the M271's timing chain concerns.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want a six‑cylinder estate without the running costs of the C350.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history or the job itself.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Balance shaft wear, intake manifold issues
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol (pre‑facelift) — smooth but watch for early issues.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Balance shaft wear, intake manifold issues
rows.ownershipVerdict: Powerful Petrol — more performance, more maintenance.
Avoid — older engine, phased out for good reason.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Injector seal leaks (Black Death), DPF clogging
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — efficient, durable, widely available.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leak, swirl flap motor failure
rows.ownershipVerdict: Powerful Diesel — V6 refinement, V6 running costs.

economicsRule.title: The S204 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against a £4,000–£12,000 S204 sits at 23–120% of the car's value. On a sub‑£5,000 S204, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 35–60% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S204 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: C220 CDI (OM651) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S204 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: C350 CDI (OM642) — V6 diesel running costs add up.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S204 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: C220 CDI (OM651) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.affectedModels: C180 CGI, C200 CGI, C250 CGI (all M271 variants)
problems.rootCause: The M271's timing chain tensioner can wear prematurely, leading to chain stretch and, if ignored, catastrophic valve‑to‑piston contact. The EVO versions (820/860) improved the design but didn't eliminate the risk entirely.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of a rattling noise on cold start, get the timing chain inspected immediately — a £600–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,800–£4,200 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M271 engine prices for your S204 petrol →

problems.title: OM651 Injector Seal Leaks (Black Death)
problems.affectedModels: C180 CDI, C220 CDI, C250 CDI (OM651)
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on the OM651 can fail, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injector. This creates a carbon buildup that sounds like a ticking noise and can eventually damage the cylinder head.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a ticking noise from the engine bay, have the injector seals checked immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows if the head is damaged.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your S204 diesel →

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Wear (pre‑facelift)
problems.rootCause: The M272's balance shaft sprocket can wear prematurely due to a manufacturing defect, leading to timing chain issues and, if ignored, catastrophic engine failure.
problems.recommendation: On pre‑facelift C300/C350 models, have the balance shaft inspected at 60,000 miles — an £800–£1,800 preventative repair is cheap insurance against a £2,500–£5,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your S204 V6 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seals (located in the V of the engine) can degrade and leak, allowing oil to mix with coolant or leak onto the engine. Repair requires significant disassembly.
problems.recommendation: If you notice oil in the coolant or a burning oil smell, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if the engine overheats.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your S204 V6 diesel →

replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — the S204 shares its engine bay with the W204 saloon, so labour times and costs are consistent across the range. Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S204 estate only. The W204 saloon and C204 coupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
